note: Habib Suliman addresses the Bahyoza , the name for residents of the Kyamutwala kingdom, where the famous bard from Kiziba often performed. Lines 1-3 are praise names associated with Mugasha and other spirits. They are quoted from the spirits' self praise poetry ( ebyebugo ), which they recite with characteristic styles when they possess spirit mediums. Bards use and conserve this knowledge. Even though there is an organization of spirit mediums, the names and praises of spirits are not standardized. Bitankwama : possibly "These-(insulting words)-do-not-bother-me," a name that fits Mugasha's style. Nyabuleza and Kashasila are female spirits, whose relationship to childbearing is probably invoked here. Never-envious (of another's child), contrasts with Mugasha's aunt (l. 78ff.). Ties up... and Thorns block the pathways are images of difficult childbirth. Ishumi 1980 reports a new mother is traditionally congratulated "there are no thorns."
A mugege tree is used in medicine for vaginal bleeding; engege is menstrual pain. The tree is also used to make charcoal for ironworking.
Lwelu is the Haya name for Lake Victoria/Nyanza, probably from okw-ela, "to be white" referring to the reflection of sunlight and sky in the water.
Bull of the Tormentors-of-the-heart signifies the strongest of the Bachwezi spirits, praise for Mugasha.

verse passage in <b> aabb of four verses (<b> a 's: 4-9.1, 10-18.1; <b> b 's: 20-25.1, 26-30.1); verse passage <b> a 's consist of verses formed in <b> aba by mirror stanzas; in the first verse in <b> aba , the pattern is formed by mirror stanzas (<b> a 's: 4-5.3, 7-9.1) composed as address to Nyambubi (4-4.4,7-7.2) + near identical lines (5-5.3, 8-9.1) contrasted with lines that are not apparently formed in a stanzaic pattern (<b> b 's: 6-6.3); in the second verse in <b> aba , the pattern is formed by mirror stanzas (<b> a 's: 10-11.1, 15-18.1) composed as near identical lines (10-10.3, 15-15.3) + lines associated by incremental repetition (11-11.1, 18-18.1) contrasted with a passage (<b> b : 12-14.1) punctuated by lines that create a pattern of <b> aab (two linegroups, 12-12.2 and 12.3-13.1, ending markedly in <i> byabula nsha "they had no effect" finalized by a linegroup, 14-14.1, which also finalizes the other stanzas in this section of the figure, cf. 11-11.1, 18-18.1); verse passage <b> b 's consist of compound stanzas in <b> aab ; two compond stanzas in <b> aab (20-23.2, 23.3-25.1) comprise the first <b> b in the overall verse passage in <b> aabb ; the first of these compound stanzas is formed in <b> aab by linegroups composed as initial subjunctive verb + preposition + name or praisename/ praisename (<b> a 's: 20-20.5 and 21-22); the linegroup that completes the figure (<b> b : 23-23.2) is itself formed in <b> aab with lines that almost repeat the former composition, <i> locutive + subjunctive + preposition + praisename/ praisename, but are finalized by a line (23.2) marked by an initial indicative verb that shifts quoted speech to narration; the second of the compound stanzas that comprise the first <b> b in the overall verse passage in <b> aabb is formed by linegroups composed as initial indicative narrative verb + preposition + name or praisename/ praisename (<b> a 's: 23.3-23.5, 24-24.4) contrasted with a linegroup that completes the figure (<b> b : 25-25.1) by echoing phrases deployed structurally elsewhere (11-11.1, 18-18.1); the compound stanza that comprises the second <b> b in the overall verse passage in <b> aabb is formed in <b> aa a<b> b by three line groups of quoted discourse, each introduced by a locutive (<b> a 's: 27-27.2, 28-28.4, 29) contrasted with a linegroup (<b> b : 30-30.1) of narrated responses to the orders and acts entailed in the previous linegroups; the second linegroup in this final pattern (28-28.4) is itself a compound stanza in <b> aab , 2 + 2 + 1, formed by couplets composed as "cut" + treename/ "carve" + wooden utensil (<b> a 's: 28-28.1, 28.2-28.3), contrasted with a single line (<b> b : 28.4) that shares subjunctive verb initial syntax, but ends with a prepositional phrase rather than a direct object.
Frenzy : Mugasha's mother's state is like being jerked about in a lively dance.
Kin-of-the-Peaceful-one is a praise name for women, people associated with Nyakalembe, "The Peaceful-one," a woman's agricultural spirit and the wife of Mugasha.
These praise names for Wamala are said to refer to serpents associated with him, who carry one another on their backs, as mothers do children.
The child fisherman is Mugasha.
The miraculous child speaks at birth. One of Mugasha's names, Nyatende (31.1), is also that of his mother's sister. It refers to sheaves of crops, apropos of his association with fertility.
Trapper (of fish) is a praise name for Mugasha.
stanza in <b> abab formed by contrast between lines that contain a double verb (<b> a 's: 37, 37.2) and lines that begin with a single verb (<b> b 's:37.1, 37.3).
stanza in <b> abab formed by contrast between lines that describe an action by mugasha (<b> a 's: 38, 38.2) and those that describe a reaction by others (<b> b 's: 38.1, 38.3).

compound stanza in <b> aab , 2 + 2 + 1, formed by couplets (<b> a 's: 39-39.1, 39.2-39.3) composed as 3rd sing verb initial/ direct quotation introduced by locutive contrasted with a single line that begins with a 3rd plural verb (<b> b : 39.4).
A cattle road is located at least 30-40 yards from a dwelling, at the end of a forecourt. It is wider than a footpath.
stanza in <b> aab formed by contrast between lines composed as subject + "that day" + verb (order reverses) (<b> a 's: 40-40.1) and one line composed only of two verbs (<b> b : 40.2).
Kin-of-the-Peaceful-One are women (12n). " The joyful one grows joyous... " are words of a women's song at weddings.

compound stanza in <b> abb , 1 + 2 + 2, formed by couplets that cohere by verb initial and locutive initial syntax, respectively (<b> b 's: 41.1-41.2, 41.3-41.4) contrasted with a single subject initial line (<b> a : 41).

stanza in <b> ab bb<b> a a<b> b bb formed by contrast between lines: composed as [child] + calls + spiritname (<b> a 's: 42.1, 43.2, 44); and lines composed as praisename alone (<b> b 's: 42.2, 43, 43.1, 44.1, 44.2, 44.3).
Silent runner praises lightning, which in traditional belief is silent and relatively harmless; thunder is thought to make noise and do damage.
Nshungu is a strong seasonal wind that brings cloud formations resembling the long horns of Ankole cattle.

compound stanza in <b> aab , 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets composed as double verb/ 0verb (<b> a 's: 45-45.1, 45.2-46) and a couplet that coheres by repetition of "I'll catch" in initial position (<b> b : 46.1-46.2).
A forgetful debtor avoids looking his creditor in the eye.
Empala is a type of catfish whose whiskers swing about like the long horns of an aggressive bull.

verse in <b> aba , 1 + 4 + 1, formed by single lines that begin "I saw" + "canoe": (<b> a 's: 48, 50) contrasted with four lines (<b> b : 48.1-49.1) that form a stanza in <b> aa a<b> b by contrast between lines with 3rd person near past verbs in (near) initial position (<b> a 's: 48.1-49) and a single line of quoted speech beginning with a locutive.
Mugasha's mother invokes the Sun in two personas: as the shining heavenly body and as the child of the Creator, Nyamuhanga.
The emergence of the upper teeth of an infant before the lower is understood as an extremely unfortunate sign. It becomes an image for any grave misfortune that may befall a child and its mother.
compound stanza in <b> abab , 1 + 2 + 1 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets that essentially repeat (<b> b 's: 52.2-52.3, 54-54.1) and single lines (<b> a 's: 52.1, 53).

compound stanza in <b> abb , 1 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets that cohere by parallelism and repetition (<b> b 's: 56.1-56.2, 57-57.1) and a single line that begins with a locutive (<b> a : 56).
I have substituted “Trapper” for “elder woman” (omukaikulu in the actual transcription) referring to the midwife, to correct what appears to be a mistake.
stanza in <b> aa aaa<b> b , formed by contrast between lines that begin with 3rd person near-past (<b> a 's: 58.1-59) and a single line of quoted speech that begins with a locutive.
Mugasha's being swallowed by the water is a laughable ruse, for he is the spirit that controls its power.

stanza in <b> a aa<b> bab formed by contrast between lines (or a linegroup) that begins with a locutive and contains an ethical judgement (<b> a 's: 63.1-63.3, 65) and lines composed as "they gave her" + fish name (<b> b 's: 64, 65.1).

compound stanza in aba, 4 + 4 + 1, by repetition and syntactic parallelism; a's (67.1-.4, 69.1) are structured as 3rd person near past verb initial; b (67.5, 68, 69) forms another figure in aba: a's are structured as “I saw he passed” + placename; b (68) begins with a prepositional phrase and ends with the subject.
compound stanza in <b> aba , 3 + 4 + 3, formed by contrast between number sequences (<b> a 's: 70-70.2, 71-71.2) and lines that refer to Wamala (<b> b 's: 70.3-70.6).

compound stanza in <b> aab , 3 + 3 + 3, by virtue of contrast between lines of narration of the wind's attack (<b> a 's: 72-72.2, 73-73.2) and those that describe the daughter's defeat and speech (<b> b 's: 74-74.2)
ff. Kagolo's praise alludes to his hot-headed and warlike behavior, which spirit mediums embody when they are possessed by him.
repeated line (75, 76.1, 78.4 and 84) finalizes passages of the winds' appearance, the girls' desertion, Mugasha's proposal and Lyanyaibungo's reply
Dry words are strong and effective, as opposed to ‘wet' words, which are weak.
Stiff Elbows probably reflects his demeanor as a possessing spirit; it seems to reinforce in gestural language his extreme and immoderate behavior.
stanza in annular structure - <b> abcba - by contrast between lines composed as aside (in distant past tense)/ locutive + 2nd person/ 0verb descriptor/ locutive + 2nd person/ aside.

verse in <b> aba formed by mirror stanzas (<b> a 's: 81-81.5, 85-86.2) composed as formulaic aside (81, 86)/ communicative act described (81.1-2, 85.1-.2)/ address (81.3, 86)/ statement involving marriage, inflected as contrary-to-fact (81.4, 86.1)/ vowed incest (81.5, 86.2) contrasted with lines composed as praise/ formulaic line (<b> b 's: 82-84).
For Big eyes...forgetful debtor see 45.1n.
Nyatende refers to Mugasha.

compound stanza in <b> abb , 1 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets (<b> a 's: 87-87.1, 87.2-87.3) each introduced by a locutive, and cohering respectively by enjambment (name/ praisename) parallelism (initial 2nd person subjunctive).
compound stanza in <b> abab ab formed between lines composed as praisename final (<b> a 's: 88, 90, 91) and those that represent drum sound (<b> b 's: 88.1-.2, 89-89.1, 90.1-.2, 91.1).

Bull denotes leadership and preeminence and is used in praise of the maiden — she is the most pleasing among maidens, like deep red wild figs, the kalaito fruit. Her stature is parallel and opposed to that of Mugasha, who is called the Bull of the Tormenters-of-the-Heart — first among the Bachwezi spirits of possession (ll. 1.6, 94.1).

ff. Lyanyaibungo was gathering straw to place on the floor of a dwelling. Work of this kind comes under the category of "to make things pleasing" a knowledge especially associated with marriagable young women.

verse passage formed by repeated sequences of three types of lines: [verb of entry or appearance] + animal name (91.2, 92, 92.3, 93, 94, 95, 97, 99.1, 99.3, 100.1, 101.2)/ praisenames of animal (91.3, 92.4, 93.1-.2, 95.1-96.1, 99.2, 102-102.1)/ locutive + "you marry her, Mugasha"
For exegesis of Kagolo's praise, see 82n.

two verse passages formed by Mugasha's general questions about brideprice and wedding gifts, respectively (103.3 and 104) and answers and Mugasha's substitutions composed in pairs as locutive + answer/ locutive + substitution (103.4-103.5, 103.6-103.7 and 104.1-104.2, 104.3-104.4, 105.1-105.2, 105.4-105.5, 106-106.1); note interpolated formulaic lines (105 and 105.3) and finalizing repetition of "confounded" (106.3).
They-die-but-they've-been-told is the name of Mugasha's drum.
Roaring here translates ngu , a conjunction sometimes used to introduce indirect discourse. Here it seems onomatopoetic.

verse passage in <b> aba , 3 + 3 + 3, formed by three triplets each composed as animal substitution/ attribute of wedding role/ permanent attribute of animal; first and third triplets are essentially the same.

Ensenene grasshoppers are caught, dressed, cooked, and eaten as a great delicacy that comes once a year, during the short rainy season. They are usually caught singly with the hand. The tasty grasshopper boasts that there will be so many as to be scooped up in large baskets.

verse passage in <b> aba , 4 + 4 + 5, formed by linegroups (<b> a 's: 113-113.3, 114.4-114.8) composed as I saw + that day + [name]/ praisename/ locutive + 2 or 3 lines that first enunciate refusal (113.2-.3) then acceptance (114.6-.8), contrasted with an intervening linegroup (<b> b : 114-114.3) that is internally formed as <b> aba by contrast between lines composed as "he sent" + direct object (<b> a 's: 114, 114.3) and lines that are formulaic repetitions (<b> b 's:114.1, 114.2).
stanza in <b> aabb bbbb formed by a couplet and a longer linegroup that cohere, respectively, by parallelism (3 verb figure) and enjambment (name / praisenames...).

compound stanza in <b> aa a<b> b , 2 + 2 + 2 + 1, formed by utterances in couplets (<b> a 's: 122.8-122.9, 122.10-123, 124-124.1) that cohere by enjambment, authorial reference (aside), and by chaining, contrasted with a single line utterance (<b> b : 124.2) that abruptly changes topic and finalizes the exchange.
compound stanza in <b> aab , 2 + 2 + 2, formed by couplets that cohere by parallelism and repetition (<b> a 's: 124.3-124.4, 124.5-124.6) contrasted with a couplet that coheres as a number sequence.
The frog's song and the events it alludes to apparently come from a folktale, but I have not been able to find the one.

stanza in <b> abab formed by contrast between lines that constitute dependent clauses (<b> a 's: 125, 125.2) and lines, each an independent clause, that are composed as [plant name] + "changed" + plant name (<b> b 's: 125.1, 125.3).

On the day before a bride leaves home to live with her husband's family, an elder representative of the husband's clan comes bearing a spear to make a speech. The fish who performs this act here is a kind of catfish, whose spear Wamara fused to his back.
The maiden of the hafted knife is a young girl who accompanies the speech-making, spear-bearing elder. Her cultural butterfat becomes natural slime by the power of Wamala.

compound stanza in <b> abab , 2 + 2 + 2 + 5, formed by contrast between couplets composed as "that day" + I saw [Kagolo]/ "he was confounded" (<b> a 's: 129-130, 131-131.1) and lines composed of praisenames (<b> b 's: 130.1-130.2, 131.2-132.2).2
Drink it at the spearshaft of Ndekezi is a proverbial expression for committing suicide. The phrase refers to a poison ordeal administered by a medium of the spirit Kimuli.

These lines are spoken in a rapid recitative. Welata is supposed to be a word in the Ganda language. Mugasha speaks Haya with a Ganda accent and uses some Ganda vocabulary, because comes from the north, from the Sesse Islands of Uganda in Lake Victoria/Nyanza.
stanza in <b> aab formed by contrast between lines that constitute dependent clauses and begin with an adverbial past perfect (<b> a 's: 137.1, 137.2) and a single line of quoted speech.

compound stanza in <b> aab , 2 + 2 + 9, formed by contrast between couplets referring to Mugasha composed as locutive + "I have come" + subject (name or praisename)/ praisename (<b> a 's: 138-138.1, 139-139.2) and lines referring to Kagolo that begin identically, but append many more praisenames (<b> b 's: 139.3-141.2).
These vocables are sung in the melody of a fisherman's paddling song.
I have nothing, nothing to compare it to , a confession of defeat: Wamala's rhetorical inability to make comparisons is equated with his lack of physical mastery.
Kagolo's power to alter natural species recalls that of Wamala.
Looked over with one eye . To look at a person briefly, with the head turned making one eye prominent communicates a hostile, or at least not a friendly, disposition.
stanza in <b> abb formed by contrast between a quoted proverb (<b> a : 147) and two lines of explication (<b> b 's: 147.1, 147.2) each composed as proper noun subject + verb.
Mother's child is a way of referring to one's sibling (here, a brother) that connotes affection.

The water oracle (Byantabuzi) is associated with this spirit (its name comes from oku-tabula, “to make water bubble up or forth”). Byantabuzi also echoes the name of the place where Kagolo acquired his characteristic way of talking (l. 143.3).
“Where could we go...?” is a question that speaks to the virtue and shame invested in the events. The lowly fisherman acts on behalf of many.
The bull of Nyambubi refers to Mugasha.
